concepts.title: Electric Dipole Moment
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Dipole Moment 

A pair of equal and opposite point charges $\pm q$, that are separated by a fixed distance is known as electric dipole. Electric dipole occurs in nature in a variety of situations The hydrogen fluoride molecule $\left( \text{HF} \right)$ is typical. When a hydrogen atom combines with a fluorine atom, the single electron of the former is strongly attracted to the latter and spends most of its time near the fluorine atom. As a result, the molecule consists of a strongly negative fluorine ion some (small) distance away from a strongly positive ion, though the molecule is electrically neutral overall.

Every electric dipole is characterized by its electric dipole moment which is a vector $\mathbf{\vec{P}}$ directed from the negative to the positive charge.

The magnitude of dipole moment is,

$\text{P}=\left( 2\text{a} \right)\text{q}$

Here, $2\text{a}$ is the distance between the two charges.

concepts.title: Electric Dipole Potential
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Dipole Potential 

The potential of an electric dipole can be found by superposing the point charge potentials of the two charges.

$\text{V}=\text{Kq}\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{r}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{r}}_{2}}} \right]=\text{Kq}\left[ \frac{{{\text{r}}_{2}}-{{\text{r}}_{1}}}{{{\text{r}}_{1}}{{\text{r}}_{2}}} \right]$

For cases where $r>d$, this can be approximated by

$\text{V}=\frac{\text{kpcos}\theta }{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

The potential of a dipole is of most interest where $r>>d$. The standard approximations are

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & {{\text{r}}_{2}}-{{\text{r}}_{1}}\approx \text{dcos}\theta \\ {} & {{\text{r}}_{1}}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\approx {{\text{r}}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

where $\vec{p}=q\vec{d}$ is defined as the dipole moment. 

Electric Potential at

(a) End on position (a point lying on the axis of the dipole)

$\theta =0$, or $\pi $

$\text{V}=\left( \pm \frac{\text{Kp}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)$

(b) Board side on position (a point lying on the perpendicular bisector $\theta =\pm \pi /2$

$\text{V}=0$

concepts.title: Electric Field Intensity Due to Dipole
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field Intensity Due to Dipole

(Note that the electric field intensity must be lying in the same plane containing the point, the axis of the dipole

Find the field along the ${{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}$ and ${{\text{E}}_{\theta }}$

$\begin{matrix} {{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{-\partial \text{V}}{\partial \text{r}}=\frac{-\partial }{\partial \text{r}}\left( \frac{\text{Kpcos}\theta }{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{Kpcos}\theta }{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right) \\ {{\text{E}}_{\theta }}=\frac{-\partial \text{V}}{\text{r}\partial \theta }=\frac{-\partial }{\partial \theta }\left( \frac{\text{Kpcos}\theta }{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{Kpsin}\theta }{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

(Note that we put variable of differentiation as $\text{r}\partial \theta $ in case of ${{\text{E}}_{\theta }}$)

$\begin{array}{l} \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{E}}_{\text{net}}} =\sqrt{\text{E}_{\text{r}}^{2}+\text{E}_{\theta }^{2}} \\ =\frac{\text{Kp}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}}\sqrt{4\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta +\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta } \\ \end{array}$

$\text{E}=\frac{\text{Kp}\sqrt{3\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta +1}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}}$

To find the direction

$\text{tan}\alpha =\left( \frac{{{\text{E}}_{\theta }}}{{{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}} \right)=\frac{\text{tan}\theta }{2}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\alpha =\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{1}{2}\text{tan}\theta \right)$ where $\alpha $ is the angle with ${{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}$.

(a) Electric field at end on position (axial position)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \theta =0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\pi , \\ {} & {{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}=\left( \frac{2\text{Kp}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\uparrow \uparrow \vec{p} \right) \\ \end{array}$

At end on position, net electric field is parallel to dipole moment direction. (b) Electric field at board side on position (equatorial position)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \theta =\pm \pi /2 \\ {} & {{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}=0,{{\text{E}}_{\theta }}=\left( \frac{\text{Kp}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{E}}_{\text{net}}}={{\text{E}}_{\theta }}=\frac{\text{Kp}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \\ {} & ~\left(\vec{E}\uparrow \downarrow \vec{p} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Net electric field is anti-parallel at broad side on position.

concepts.title: Torque Experienced by Dipole in a Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Torque Experienced by Dipole in a Field

Torque experienced by an electric dipole placed is an uniform electric field ${{\text{F}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ [Equal and opposite forces$]$

These forces which are equal and opposite form couple.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\tau & ~=\text{qE}\left( \text{dsin}\theta \right) \\ {} & ~=\text{P}\left(\text{sin}\theta \right)\text{E} \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \vec{\tau}=\vec{P}\times \vec{E} \\ \end{array}$

Remarks about couple and characteristics

(i) It remains same about whichever point you calculate the torque

(ii) Couple is said to be formed when equal and opposite forces are acting on the body separated by same distance

(iii) Couple is a free vector

concepts.title: Potential Energy of Dipole
concepts.conceptBody:
Potential Energy of Dipole 

Work done against the field in rotating slowly the dipole from $\theta =90$ to $\theta $.

Note: $\text{U}=0$ (assumed) at an angle $\pi /2$ from $\vec{E}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{dU} ~=-\tau \left| \text{d}\theta \right| \\ \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U} ~=+\text{pE}\mathop{\int }_{\pi /2}^{\theta }\text{sin}\theta \text{d}\theta \\ {} ~=-\text{pE}|\text{cos}\theta |_{\pi /2}^{\theta } \\ \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U} ~=-\text{pEcos}\theta \\ {{\text{U}}_{\theta }}-{{\text{U}}_{\pi /2}} ~=\text{pEcos}\theta \\ {{\text{U}}_{\pi /2}} ~=0 \\ {{\text{U}}_{\theta }} ~=-\text{pEcos}\theta =-\text{p}.\text{E} \\ \end{array}$

A point $P$ is at a distance of $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ from this origin such that $O P$ makes an angle ${ }^{\frac{\pi}{3}}$ with the $x$-axis. If the electric field at $P$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis, the value of $\theta$ would be

Two opposite and equal charges $4 \times 10^{-8}$ coulomb when placed $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm}$ away, form a dipole. If this dipole is placed in an external electric field $4 \times 10^8$ newton/coulomb, the value of maximum torque and the work done in rotating it through $180^{\circ}$ will be

An electric dipole when placed in a uniform electric field $\boldsymbol{E}$ will have minimum potential energy, if the positive direction of dipole moment makes the following angle with $\boldsymbol{E}$
